exercises.title: A minimal AI image
exercises.prompt: Write a Dockerfile that starts from a Python base, installs a couple of dependencies from `requirements.txt`, copies in a script, and runs it. Build it and run the container.
exercises.hint: Order layers cheap-to-expensive: copy requirements and install *before* copying your code, so code edits don't bust the dependency cache.
exercises.solution:
```dockerfile
FROM python:3.11-slim
WORKDIR /app
COPY requirements.txt .
RUN pip install --no-cache-dir -r requirements.txt
COPY . .
CMD ["python", "main.py"]
```
```bash
docker build -t my-ai-app .
docker run --rm my-ai-app
```

Copying `requirements.txt` and installing *before* `COPY . .` means editing your code reuses the cached dependency layer — rebuilds take seconds instead of reinstalling everything.

exercises.title: Persist models with a volume
exercises.prompt: Run a container that downloads/writes a model file, and arrange things so that file survives `docker run` again without re-downloading. Prove the file is still there on the host after the container exits.
exercises.hint: Bind-mount a host directory to the container path your code writes to (`-v $(pwd)/models:/app/models`). Container filesystems are ephemeral; volumes are not.
exercises.solution:
```bash
mkdir -p models
docker run --rm -v "$(pwd)/models:/app/models" my-ai-app \
  python -c "open('/app/models/weights.bin','wb').write(b'x'*1024)"
ls -lh models/weights.bin    # still on the host after the container is gone
```

Without the `-v` mount, anything written inside the container vanishes when it exits (`--rm`). Mounting a host dir means downloaded weights, datasets, and checkpoints persist across rebuilds — essential when a model is many GB.

exercises.title: Two services with Compose
exercises.prompt: Write a `docker-compose.yml` that runs two services that talk to each other — an inference app and a vector database — on the same network, with the DB's data persisted. Explain how the app addresses the DB.
exercises.hint: Compose puts services on one network; each is reachable by its service name as hostname. Use a named volume for the DB.
exercises.solution:
```yaml
services:
  app:
    build: .
    ports: ["8000:8000"]
    environment:
      - QDRANT_URL=http://vectordb:6333   # service name = hostname
    depends_on: [vectordb]
  vectordb:
    image: qdrant/qdrant:latest
    volumes:
      - qdrant_data:/qdrant/storage
volumes:
  qdrant_data:
```
```bash
docker compose up -d --build
```

Compose creates a shared network where each service is reachable by its **service name** (`app` reaches the DB at `http://vectordb:6333`) — no IPs, no host networking. The named volume `qdrant_data` keeps the index across `compose down`/`up`. This is the same pattern your own site uses to add the license-service container.
